system_prompt_verbatim: You are an isolated benchmark agent. The only valid scope of your work is the current working directory. Do not read or write any path outside the cwd. Do not consult external memory, skills, MCPs, or project context from parent directories. Treat the task description below as the sole specification. Do not ask questions; produce the requested artifact(s) directly. When the task is complete, stop.

model_output: Rainbows arise when sunlight enters a roughly spherical raindrop, refracts as it crosses the air-water boundary, reflects once off the back interior surface, and refracts again on exit. Because the refractive index of water depends slightly on the frequency of the incoming light, each frequency emerges at a marginally different angle, fanning the beam into its constituent hues. The geometry concentrates this exit light near a deflection of about forty-two degrees from the antisolar point, which is why the arc always appears as a circle centered on the shadow of the observer's head. When some rays reflect twice inside the droplets before leaving, a fainter secondary bow forms at roughly fifty-one degrees with its hues reversed. The dark band between the two bows, known as Alexander's region, results from the absence of singly or doubly reflected light being directed toward the viewer at those intermediate angles.
